actions.thought: T6o is a weak hand to open from the Button, even in a 3-handed game. I prefer to wait for better spots and hands that have better post-flop playability to minimize variance early in the tournament.

As the Small Blind with J4s after the button has folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. While J4s is a weak holding, the positional advantage and the potential to pick up the pot pre-flop justify an aggressive approach. Raising to 300 is a standard maneuver to apply pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot immediately.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, A2 offsuit is generally too weak to defend. It plays poorly post-flop and lacks the equity to call, and while it has an Ace blocker, 3-bet bluffing without reads on Hand 1 is unnecessary. Folding to preserve our stack for better spots.

With Ace-Seven suited on the button in a three-handed game, I hold a strong positional advantage and a hand with good equity. Opening to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard aggressive move to either steal the blinds or take control of the pot with initiative.

Facing a 3x button raise from Flashlight, I'm out of position in the small blind with a suited one-gapper (8s 6s). Calling here is generally unprofitable due to the positional disadvantage, the large raise size, and the risk of a squeeze from the Big Blind. While a 3-bet bluff is an option occasionally, the standard and safest play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 300, which is a 3x raise. Pro in the Small Blind folded. To call, I need to put in 200 more chips into a pot that will be 650, giving me roughly 30.7% pot odds. K7o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind with against a wide Button opening range in a 3-handed game, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. I will call to see a flop.

I have middle pair (Kings) on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard play. This allows me to evaluate the strength of Flashlight's hand based on their betting action. I have decent showdown value but should be cautious of Aces.

I hold top pair on a dry board and maintain the betting lead. Since the Big Blind checked to me, a continuation bet of roughly one-third to half pot is the standard play to gain value from weaker hands and prevent my opponent from realizing equity with a free card. 250 chips into a 650 pot is a solid size to balance value and protection.

I have middle pair (Kings) on a board with an Ace and a King. Flashlight's bet is small (less than half pot). My hand is too strong to fold to a single continuation bet, as it beats all bluffs and many potential pocket pairs or weaker draws. Raising doesn't achieve much since it's unlikely a better hand (like an Ace) will fold, and I want to keep their bluffs in. I will call to see the turn.

I have middle pair (Kings) on a board that now has an Ace and a Ten. The Ten is a somewhat coordinated card, potentially improving Flashlight's range (e.g., QJ, KT, AT). Checking is the most prudent action here to control the pot size and gauge Flashlight's strength. I'm not strong enough to lead out for value, and I'd like to see if they check behind or continue betting.

With top pair and a weak kicker on a board that is becoming increasingly coordinated, checking behind is the prudent play. Betting here risks isolating myself against hands that beat me, such as better Aces or two-pair combinations, or getting check-raised by a draw. Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep the opponent's range wide, potentially allowing me to get to a cheaper showdown or evaluate a river action.

I have middle pair (Kings) on a board with an Ace. Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) checked the turn, which often indicates they don't have a strong Ace or are pot-controlling. The river 5d is a brick. I have significant showdown value but unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet. Checking allows me to reach showdown or potentially catch a bluff from a missed draw (like QJ).

I hold a pair of Aces with a seven kicker. The board of Ac-Ks-Th-5d-2d is relatively dry and lacks any flush possibilities. Given that my opponent has checked the river, they are likely weak, potentially holding a busted draw, a weak pair, or checking a medium-strength hand. Betting now provides little value as worse hands are unlikely to call, and I risk being check-raised by a stronger hand that may have been trapping. Therefore, checking behind is the safest path to showdown, allowing me to realize my equity and win the pot against a wider range of hands.
74o is a very weak offsuit hand. As the first to act on the Button pre-flop, this is an easy fold. I will save my chips for a hand with better playability and equity.

With the button folding, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation. Queen-Eight offsuit is a reasonable hand to open-raise with from the Small Blind to apply pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. Even though I am closing the action and getting reasonable pot odds, the hand has poor equity and will be difficult to navigate post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9h Ts. In a 3-handed tournament, this is a solid hand to open for a raise. Standard opening size here has been 300 (3x the big blind), so I will maintain consistency with previous betting patterns to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand with position.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 8s, which is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and offsuit. Calling a raise out of position would be mathematically negative expected value, and I have no reason to 3-bet bluff at this stage. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack.
K4 offsuit is too weak to defend from the big blind against a 3x raise from the button, especially out of position. Folding is the standard and optimal play to preserve my stack.

9c 3h is a weak, unsuited hand with no board coverage and very low equity. Playing this hand from the button is negative expected value, as it is difficult to play in a multi-way pot. Folding is the most disciplined and standard strategy.

Action folds to me in the Small Blind. I hold Q7 offsuit, which is a weak and marginal hand. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with this holding is generally unprofitable. Folding is the safest and most standard decision.

J9o is an offsuit one-gapper, which is quite marginal to open even from the button in a 3-handed game. While raising to steal the blinds is a possibility, it often leads to tough post-flop decisions when called by stronger ranges. Folding is a solid, conservative play early in the tournament to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 6c. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. Q6s is a relatively strong hand in a head-up blind vs blind situation. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play the hand with initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-2 suited, which is a bottom-tier hand with very poor equity. Even though the price to call is decent, I am out of position against Flash who has been aggressive. It is better to fold and preserve my stack rather than chasing a marginal spot with a weak hand.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While this is a borderline hand that could be used as a stealing candidate in a three-handed game, I have been quite active in the opening hands of this tournament (raising in Hands 3, 4, and 6). To maintain a balanced image and avoid getting involved in a potentially bloated pot with a weak hand out of position against either the Small Blind or the Big Blind, I will fold this hand and wait for a more favorable spot.

With the button folding, I am heads-up in the small blind against the big blind. Holding Queen-high, I have a reasonable hand to play aggressively in this short-handed scenario. By raising to 300, I put pressure on the big blind to defend or fold, aiming to win the pot immediately.

Facing a 3bb open from the Small Blind, A2o is a weak holding. Even though we have position post-flop, it is frequently dominated by better Aces and has poor overall playability. Calling here is marginal at best and 3-bet bluffing is unnecessary, so folding is the standard and safest decision to preserve our stack early in the tournament.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game, which is the most advantageous position. Although 10s 2s is a relatively weak hand, opening from the button is essential to maintain aggression and steal the blinds. I have been consistent with a 300-chip open raise, so I will stick to that sizing to maintain my table image.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind. Ten-Nine offsuit is too weak to flat call out of position and not strong enough to 3-bet for value. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve the stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q9o. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, which is a standard steal position. Given the pot odds (200 to call for a 450 pot), Q9o is a mathematically sound hand to defend with. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my hand's strength then.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a nine kicker on a board of K-Q-5. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard move to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts. I am looking to check-call most reasonable bets but don't want to lead into the raiser here.

I am in position with absolute air (two-high with a backdoor flush draw). The board of 5-Q-K is very favorable to the Big Blind's defending range, which likely contains many kings, queens, and straight draws. Betting here into a calling range is unprofitable and high-variance. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and take a free card, which is the most prudent play given my lack of equity.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a nine kicker. On the flop, the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) checked back, which reduces the likelihood that they hold a King or a better Queen. The four of diamonds on the turn is a blank. I will check again to control the pot and potentially induce a bluff from Flashlight's weaker range or see a cheap showdown, as leading out now might fold out hands I currently beat.

I am holding 10-high with a weak gutshot draw on a 5-Q-K-4 board. Since I checked the flop, I have no reason to start betting now. I lack showdown value, and checking back is the most conservative and standard play to control the pot size and reach the river cheaply without committing more chips with a dominated hand.

On the river, the second Queen appears, giving me three of a kind (Queens). Flashlight (the pre-flop raiser) has checked both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they do not have a King or a high-equity hand. Since I have improved significantly and the board is relatively safe (the only hands beating me are KQ, which is unlikely given the action, or the case Queen), I will lead out with a value bet. A bet of 300 into a 650 pot is designed to get called by Ace-high, small pocket pairs, or a weak King that played cautiously.

I am facing a bet of 300 into a 650 pot on the river with 10-high on a board of 5-Q-K-4-Q. My hand has absolutely no showdown value, and even if my opponent is bluffing with a weaker hand or a missed draw, my 10-high loses to most of their range, including any Ace-high or pair. There is no incentive to call here as I am strictly behind, and raising would be a massive overplay with no equity. The only sound decision is to fold.

Pocket threes on the button is a solid starting hand, particularly for set mining or stealing the blinds. I will make a standard 3 big blind raise to 300. This size applies pressure to the blinds and allows me to play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind holding 97 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for a 3x raise. 97o is a weak hand to play out of position against an open raise, especially with the Big Blind still to act. It is most profitable to fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Ten suited. Pro raised from the button to 300, and Flash folded. I have already posted 100 as the big blind, so I need to call 200 to see the flop. With a pot of 450 (300 from Pro, 50 from Flash, 100 from me), calling is mathematically sound given the pot odds and my holding. Three-betting is an option, but with 150 hands to play, I prefer to keep the pot controlled and play this hand post-flop in position.

The board (2d, 7c, 7d) is dry and unlikely to have connected well with my Queen-Ten offsuit. As the big blind, I am first to act on this flop. With only Queen-high and a backdoor diamond draw, betting into the pre-flop aggressor is high-risk with low reward. I will check to keep the pot controlled and see how the opponent reacts, planning to fold to a significant continuation bet.

On this paired board (7-7-2), my pocket threes are often the best hand, but they are very vulnerable to overcards. By making a continuation bet, I can deny equity to two unsuited overcards and often take down the pot right here. Betting around 1/3 of the pot is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

The opponent has made a small bet of 200 into a pot of 850, which provides excellent pot odds (roughly 19%). While my Queen-Ten high is currently weak, the board is dry and the small sizing suggests the opponent might be probing or holding a marginal hand. With backdoor potential, I have enough equity to justify a call to see the turn card.

The Jack of Diamonds on the turn improves my hand to a flush draw with the Queen of Diamonds. Being out of position, checking is the most strategic approach to control the pot and see how the opponent reacts to the turn card. If Pro bets, I have sufficient pot odds to consider calling given the potential for a flush on the river, and if Pro checks back, I get a free card to potentially improve my hand.

With the Jack of diamonds arriving on the turn, the board now has three diamonds and a J, heavily connecting with hands that would call my flop bet (like flush draws, overcards, or a floated Jack). My pocket threes are now mostly likely behind. Although I hold the 3 of diamonds for a weak flush draw, betting here has almost no value as I will only be called by better hands. Checking behind to control the pot size and realize equity for free is the optimal decision.

The river Queen of Clubs pairs my hole card, giving me a pair of queens. Pro checked the turn, which suggests they lack a strong made hand like a set of sevens or a completed flush. Betting now would likely only get called by hands that beat me or cause them to fold their bluffs. Checking behind is the best way to get to showdown safely and potentially win against their weaker holdings.

With the Queen arriving on the river, the board is even scarier (2d 7c 7d Jd Qc) and misses my hand entirely. My pocket threes have very limited showdown value but absolutely no value to bet, as I will only be called by better hands like a higher pair, flush, or trip sevens. Bluffing is also risky against Flashlight's calling range, which could easily contain a Queen, Jack, or medium pocket pair that floated the flop. Checking behind is the best decision to take the hand to showdown for free and minimize losses.

I am on the button with 9d 4s. This is a very weak hand with poor post-flop equity and low high-card value. While standard button opening ranges in 3-handed play are wide, 94 offsuit is generally at the bottom of the range and a clear fold to maintain a disciplined strategy. I have 10,150 chips and there is no need to risk them on such a weak hand when I can wait for better spots.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Eight offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against the big blind. Since the button folded, I have the option to complete, but completing with such a marginal hand out of position is generally a losing play. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable decision here to preserve my stack.

In a three-handed game, holding 5c4c on the button gives me a strong position. I want to maintain the aggressive playstyle that has worked for me in previous hands, and attempting a steal of the blinds with a small raise is a standard and profitable move given the relatively shallow blinds.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, 87s is a marginal hand to play out of position. Calling leaves us vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind and forces us to play a bloated pot out of position without the initiative. The 3x sizing makes the pot odds less favorable for a call. While a 3-bet is an option, folding is the lowest variance and most standard play to preserve our stack early in the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with a premium hand, Ace-Queen suited. Flashlight opened from the button, which is often a wide range in 3-handed play. To protect my hand, build the pot, and take the initiative while being out of position for the rest of the hand, I will put in a 3-bet. A raise to 1000 is standard to put pressure on the opener.

My opening raise with 5c4c was a standard button steal attempt. Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, this hand is too weak to continue, especially given the size of the 3-bet. While I have position, folding is the most disciplined and conservative play to preserve my stack size in this tournament.
KTo is a solid starting hand on the button in a 3-handed game. Opening to 300 (3x the big blind) is the standard play to steal the blinds or play a pot in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 2s, which is a very weak hand. Pro has raised to 300 from the Button. Calling out of position with such a low-equity hand is a losing play in the long run, and there is no reason to attempt a 3-bet bluff here given the stack depths and the strength of a standard button opening range. Folding is the most disciplined choice to conserve my stack.

I hold Jack-Five offsuit in the big blind, which is a weak hand to defend against an open-raise from the button. Even with the offered pot odds, playing a dominated hand out of position is strategically unsound. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 73s. While 3-handed play requires a wider opening range, 73s is near the bottom of the deck and doesn't provide enough equity or playability to justify an open raise or a call against the small and big blinds. I've been active in previous hands, so I'll take this opportunity to fold and maintain a disciplined image.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand heads-up against the Big Blind. With the button having folded, this is an ideal spot to raise and assert control over the pot, forcing the Big Blind to either fold or commit more chips with a weaker range.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. While Ten-Seven suited has some playability and we would have position post-flop, it's still a marginal holding. Given our stack depth and early stage of the match, avoiding potentially tricky spots with marginal hands is prudent. Folding is standard here to wait for better opportunities.

Holding 2c 5c on the button, my hand is extremely weak and lacks any significant playability or equity. It is not profitable to invest chips in this spot, especially considering the strength of a potential steal attempt is diminished by such a low-value hand. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

I have 73 offsuit in the small blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Since it's folded to me, I have no reason to play this hand, so folding is the optimal and standard decision here to preserve my stack.

T2o is an extremely weak hand. Even though I am on the button, this hand has virtually no playability or equity to justify an open raise or call. The optimal move is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

Action is folded to me in the small blind. Ace-Five offsuit is a strong hand in three-handed play, especially when folded to. I have a healthy stack and want to exert pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to either take the pot immediately or play with initiative post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen suited, a premium hand that plays well against almost any opening range. Given the opponent's aggressive tendencies in this tournament, simply calling would allow them to see a flop cheaply with potential air or medium cards. A 3-bet is the most strategic play here to assert control, define my range, and potentially take down the pot immediately or build a larger pot with a strong hand in position for the post-flop play. Raising to 900 represents a standard 3-bet size that forces the opponent to make a difficult decision with their likely wide raising range.

I opened to 3x from the small blind with Ace-Five offsuit, and the big blind responded with a 3-bet to 900. While I have an Ace blocker, playing A5o out of position in a 3-bet pot is generally a losing proposition. I would either have to 4-bet bluff or call and play a bloated pot without initiative or position. Given the early stage of the tournament and my current chip lead, I prefer to fold and avoid high-variance situations with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Qd 9s in a three-handed game. Q9o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. In previous hands, I have consistently used 300 as my opening raise size, so I will continue with that sizing to maintain range balance and steal the blinds.

I am in the small blind with 6d-5h, which is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Being out of position against an aggressive button raiser makes this an easy fold. There is no strategic value in defending this hand given the poor playability and lack of equity.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand (J3o) facing a 3bb raise from the Button. This hand has very poor equity and playability post-flop. The correct decision is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button holding 4s 6d, which is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. Opening with this hand is negative expected value, even with position, as it does not play well post-flop. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

With 6c 3s in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Playing from out of position with such poor equity is a losing proposition, so the optimal decision is to simply fold.

85s is a weak suited two-gapper. Even from the Button, it is generally too weak to open against opponents who have shown aggression and 3-bet tendencies. Folding is the most straightforward and solid play.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 6c, a very weak offsuit hand. The button (Pro) has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Flashlight has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Given the low strength of my hand and the disadvantage of being out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. My previous raise size has consistently been 300 (3x the big blind), and I will maintain that sizing to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early.

With Ace-Eight offsuit, I have a playable hand against a button open in a 3-handed game. While playing out of position as the small blind is disadvantageous, folding is too tight given the hand strength and the frequency of button opens. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my position, keeping the pot manageable.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand, facing a 3x raise from the Button and a flat call from the Small Blind. Calling here would mean playing a bloated pot multi-way, out of position relative to the pre-flop aggressor, with a hand that suffers from terrible reverse implied odds (easily dominated by better Kings). Folding is the only profitable long-term decision.

I am in the Small Blind with an Ace-high flush draw on a very wet board of 2d-3d-4d. Since I am out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most strategic play. I have the nut flush draw (Ah), which gives me significant equity to continue against almost any bet. Checking allows me to control the pot size and observe how my opponent reacts to the board, while preparing to either call or potentially check-raise if the situation warrants it.

I hold the nut flush draw (Ace of Diamonds) and two overcards. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Flashlight checked, a continuation bet is appropriate. Betting here allows me to take the pot down immediately or build a larger pot for when I hit my flush. The Ad is a crucial blocker, reducing the likelihood that my opponent has already flopped a flush.

My hand (Ace-High with no diamond) has missed this flop completely. The board (2d-3d-4d) is extremely connected and wet, likely hitting the opponent's opening range significantly. While my previous thoughts misidentified my hand as a flush draw, in reality, I hold no diamond, leaving me with only a weak gutshot straight draw (needing a five) and Ace-High. With the opponent betting into a dry pot, my equity is insufficient to justify a call. Folding is the only mathematically sound decision.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game, which is an extremely strong position. 5d 3d is a suited connector, which has good playability. Even though it is a low connector, I should be opening a wide range on the button to steal the blinds and maintain initiative. A standard raise to 300 is an appropriate sizing to pressure the blinds.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button while in the Small Blind with T7s. Though suited, this hand is marginal and we would be playing out of position against the pre-flop raiser, with the Big Blind still left to act. Calling often leads to tough spots and 3-betting T7s is unnecessarily loose. Folding is the standard and safest decision here to preserve the stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 3x from the button. While 3-handed play requires a wider defending range, T5o is simply too weak to defend out of position against a standard open. It has poor playability and realization of equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Queen-Eight suited is a solid starting hand from the Button in a 3-handed game. By open-raising to 300, I apply pressure to the blinds to win the pot outright while holding a hand that can flop well if called.

I hold ATo in the Small Blind. In a 3-handed game, this is a premium hand. Pro opened from the button, which is often a wide range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take initiative, punish potential steals, and potentially end the hand right now. This sizing is consistent with my previous 3-bet in Hand #11.

QTo offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a 3-bet from the Small Blind. A 3-bet from the SB typically indicates a strong range, and calling here would require me to play a bloated pot out of position against both the original raiser and the 3-better. It is mathematically and strategically safer to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I open-raised with Q8 suited from the Button, but Flash 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000. Q8 suited is far behind a standard 3-betting range, being heavily dominated by hands like AQ, KQ, and overpairs. Even though I would have position post-flop, investing another 700 chips here is unprofitable against a strong range. Folding is the optimal choice.

I am on the button with a strong pocket pair, Eight of Clubs and Eight of Hearts. In a three-handed game, this is a premium hand. Raising here is the standard play to build a pot and potentially take down the blinds. I will make it 300, which has been my standard opening size in previous hands.

I am in the Small Blind with K-J suited, a strong hand that plays well in aggressive confrontations. The Button (Flash) has been opening frequently, and I want to take the initiative and isolate them or take down the pot pre-flop. 3-betting to 1000 is a standard, strong sizing that I have seen used successfully in previous hands (#11, #21) to discourage passive play and define the ranges of my opponents.

I am in the Big Blind with AJo. The Button raised to 300 and the Small Blind 3-bet to 1000. Calling a 3-bet cold out of position with an offsuit broadway hand like AJo is generally unprofitable, as I will often be dominated by hands like AK, AQ, or premium pairs. Furthermore, the initial raiser (Button) is still yet to act behind me and could easily 4-bet. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

I have pocket eights, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I opened for 300 from the button and faced a 3-bet to 1000 from Flashlight in the small blind. While 4-betting is an option, calling is generally more effective here because it allows me to play a pot in position and realize my equity. 88 is strong enough to call a 3-bet, as it flops sets well and can often be the best hand on low-card boards. I will call the additional 700 chips.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board (Ad-6h-Td) is favorable for my 3-betting range, which includes many combinations of Ace-x. My hand, King-Jack of clubs, provides a gutshot straight draw and two overcards, giving me sufficient equity to continue the aggression. Betting 800 into a 2100 pot (approximately 38%) is a standard continuation bet sizing that maintains pressure, denies equity to weaker hands, and represents a strong range on an Ace-high board.

I hold pocket eights, which is now a marginal hand on this Ace-high board (Ad-6h-Td). My opponent 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading out with a continuation bet of 800 into a 2900 pot. While the price is attractive (needing about 22% equity), this board texture heavily favors a 3-bettor's range, which includes hands like AA, TT, AK, AQ, AJ, and AT. My eights are currently third pair and are unlikely to improve significantly on future streets. Calling here likely leads to difficult decisions on the turn if the opponent continues their aggression, and many cards (any Diamond, K, Q, J) further weaken my relative hand strength. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Holding 9h6d is a weak, disconnected, offsuit hand with very low equity. As the chip leader, there is no need to play marginal hands, especially when opening from the button with low potential. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve stack size.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand (2c 4s) after the Button folds. Calling or raising with this hand out of position is a losing play over time. The best action is to fold and preserve my chips for better opportunities.

We are first to act on the Button with 2d 3d. This is a very weak suited connector, too weak to open-raise even from the button in a 3-handed game. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded. While it is tempting to attack the Big Blind (Flashlight), Q6o is a weak hand to open with, especially since Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #15 and Hand #22). I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my stack.

I am on the button with 8-5 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While opening from the button is generally aggressive in three-handed play, this specific hand lacks the equity or playability to justify a raise or a call against the blinds. I will fold and wait for a stronger range to commit chips.

I am in the small blind with King-Jack offsuit, which is a strong hand for heads-up play against the big blind. Since the button has folded, I have a great opportunity to apply pressure. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to take down the pot or gain initiative in the hand.

Q2o is a very weak offsuit hand. Although I would have position on the Small Blind post-flop, calling a 3bb raise with such a disconnected and low-equity hand is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

Jack-four offsuit is a weak hand with poor connectivity and minimal showdown value. In a three-handed game, opening this hand from the button is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. This is a solid starting hand for a heads-up confrontation against the big blind. Raising takes the initiative and puts pressure on Flash to defend with marginal hands.

In a blind vs blind situation, Q9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard 3x open from the Small Blind. I have position on Pro for the remainder of the hand, making a call profitable given the pot odds.
